title: Descaling a shower head
subtitle: Remove limescale build-up to restore full spray coverage
excerpt: How to remove and descale a shower head: detaching the head, disassembling the spray plate, soaking in white vinegar or a proprietary descaler, clearing blocked nozzles, and reassembling for full flow.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white or off-white calcium carbonate deposit left by hard water on any surface it contacts. In a shower head, limescale blocks the spray nozzles over time, reducing the spray pattern to a few jets. White vinegar or citric acid dissolves it without damaging the shower head.

A small plastic disc inside the shower arm connection that limits the flow rate to comply with water pressure regulations. It can trap debris and limescale. If flow remains poor after descaling the head, the flow restrictor may need cleaning or removal.

content.text: Hard water leaves
limescale

inside every shower nozzle, gradually reducing the spray pattern to a few strong jets as the blocked nozzles redirect all the pressure. Descaling takes less than an hour and does not require turning off the water supply for most shower heads.
Step 1, remove the shower head

Wrap a cloth around the shower arm nut to protect the chrome finish, then unscrew the head with an adjustable spanner. Turn anticlockwise. If the head has been on for many years and the thread is seized with limescale, apply a small amount of white vinegar to the thread and leave for 10 minutes before attempting to unscrew. Do not use excessive force; an old chrome arm can shear at the wall fitting.
Step 2, disassemble and inspect
Many shower heads have a removable spray face held by a central screw. Remove it and inspect the nozzles from both sides. Check the
flow restrictor
at the inlet connection: on some heads it is a small rubber disc with a hole in the centre. If it is coated in scale, remove and soak it separately.
Step 3, soak in white vinegar

Place the spray plate and any removable parts in a bowl of undiluted white vinegar. Leave for at least 1 hour; severely scaled heads benefit from an overnight soak. For a head that cannot be fully disassembled, fill a zip-lock bag with vinegar and tie it around the head so the nozzle face is fully submerged. Citric acid solution (30 g per litre) is an alternative that works equally well.
Step 4, clear nozzles and rinse

After soaking, scrub the nozzle face with a soft brush or an old toothbrush to remove loosened scale. Push a wooden toothpick or blunt pin through any blocked nozzles from the back; do not use a metal point that could enlarge the nozzle opening permanently. Rinse all parts under a running tap.
Step 5, reassemble and refit

Reassemble the spray plate. Apply 3 to 4 wraps of PTFE tape to the shower arm thread before screwing the head back on; the old thread seal is often damaged when the head is removed. Hand-tighten then give a further quarter-turn with the spanner (cloth-wrapped). Turn on the shower and check all nozzles are spraying. If some remain blocked, disassemble and soak for a further 2 hours.
Common mistakes
Using bleach instead of acid.
Bleach does not dissolve limescale; it bleaches the scale white but does not remove it. Use white vinegar, citric acid, or a proprietary descaler.
Soaking rubber seals in vinegar for more than 4 hours.
Prolonged acid exposure degrades rubber. Remove rubber washers and seals before soaking and clean them separately with a cloth.
Refitting without replacing the PTFE tape.
The old PTFE tape is compressed and torn when the head is removed. New tape is required to seal the thread against drips at the arm connection.
